title: Self-RAG: Reflect Before You Answer
concept: The model critiques its own retrieved context and generated answer, deciding whether to retrieve more or revise.

exercises.prompt: Before we dive into Self-RAG, what is the primary purpose of basic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?

options.text: Retrieving external documents to ground the model's answer
Training the model on larger graphics cards
Using voice recognition to write code

exercises.explain: In LLM applications, RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is widely used to ground model answers in external documents to prevent hallucinations. Self-RAG builds on this to add self-correction.

cards.title: Why Double-Check?
cards.body: Standard RAG blindly trust whatever information is retrieved and displays the first answer it drafts. But what if the retrieved documents are irrelevant, or the model hallucinates while writing the answer? We need a way for the AI to double-check its own work.

cards.title: What is Self-RAG?
cards.body: Self-RAG (Self-Reflective Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is a framework where an LLM critiques its own retrieved search results and its own generated answers, grading them to decide if it needs to retrieve more information or rewrite its response.

cards.title: The Tasting Chef
cards.body: Think of Self-RAG like a chef who tastes their soup at every step. Instead of just throwing ingredients in a bowl and serving it, the chef tastes the broth, decides if it needs more salt (more retrieval), and tastes the final spoonful before letting it leave the kitchen.

cards.title: Self-RAG in Action
cards.body: If you ask Self-RAG about a rare bird species, it first outputs a token deciding it needs search results. It reads the results, outputs a token grading them as highly relevant, writes the answer, and finally outputs a token confirming the answer matches the facts.

cards.title: Myth: Post-Generation Editing
cards.body: Many think reflection happens after the entire process is finished. In reality, Self-RAG reflects on the fly during generation, outputting special 'reflection tokens' to grade its own progress at each step.

cards.title: Blind vs. Reflective
cards.body: Compare how standard RAG and Self-RAG handle the retrieved data before generating a response.

left.text: Retrieves documents and blindly writes the answer using them immediately.

right.text: Evaluates the documents first, deciding if they are actually helpful before writing.

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between standard RAG and Self-RAG?

options.text: Standard RAG is much faster because it never uses an external database.
Self-RAG critiques the retrieved documents and its own answer, while standard RAG does not.
Standard RAG can only run on local computers, while Self-RAG runs on the web.
Self-RAG completely skips the retrieval step and guesses.

exercises.explain: Standard RAG assumes retrieved documents are always useful, whereas Self-RAG actively evaluates the quality and relevance of the retrieved data.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Self-RAG can decide to fetch more documents if it finds the initial retrieved documents irrelevant.
exercises.explain: Self-RAG can trigger new retrievals dynamically if it evaluates the current retrieved context as insufficient or irrelevant.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of Self-RAG's decision mechanism.
exercises.sentence: To make decisions on the fly, Self-RAG generates reflection tokens that evaluate the relevance of retrieved documents.
exercises.explain: Self-RAG uses reflection tokens to flag whether retrieval is needed and to grade the quality of its own outputs.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario demonstrates an AI utilizing Self-RAG principles?

options.text: An AI searches a vector database for 'quantum computing' and summarizes the first three articles it finds without evaluating them.
An AI receives a query, searches the web, and pastes the top search result directly as its final answer.
An AI retrieves three documents, generates a token indicating two are irrelevant, discards the bad ones, and searches again for better sources.

exercises.explain: Option C represents a self-reflective process where the AI critiques its own retrieval quality and modifies its behavior based on that critique. Options A and B are simple sequential or standard retrieval methods.

exercises.prompt: How does Self-RAG technically implement its reflection process during generation?

options.text: It automatically deletes the user's prompt if it is too difficult
It uses self-reflection tokens to decide when to retrieve, critique retrieved facts, and rate the final response
It forces the user to manually grade every single piece of retrieved data
It replaces the LLM entirely with a simple lookup database

exercises.explain: Self-RAG uses special self-reflection tokens (like whether retrieval is needed, if the retrieved facts support the answer, and if the answer is useful) to dynamically control its generation flow, making it self-correcting.
